edges.description: Loss of RPGR impairs ciliary delivery of outer-segment cargo, including opsin-rich membrane components needed to maintain photoreceptor architecture.
edges.source: RPGR dysfunction at the photoreceptor connecting cilium

edges.description: The common trafficking defect branches clinically into rod-predominant or cone-predominant disease depending on RPGR variant context, especially position within ORF15.
Both rod- and cone-predominant branches eventually converge on severe retinal degeneration with peripheral-predominant tissue loss and inner-retinal remodeling.
edges.target: Progressive photoreceptor loss and retinal remodeling

edges.description: RPGRORF15 variation is associated with high myopia, which modifies visual-acuity decline across RPGR retinal-dystrophy branches.
Rod-predominant photoreceptor dysfunction produces childhood nyctalopia in the retinitis pigmentosa branch.
edges.source: Progressive photoreceptor loss and retinal remodeling

edges.description: Progressive retinal degeneration constricts the peripheral visual field in the rod-cone branch.
edges.source: Progressive photoreceptor loss and retinal remodeling

edges.description: Cone and macular involvement downstream of RPGR retinal degeneration drives progressive acuity loss.
edges.source: Progressive photoreceptor loss and retinal remodeling

edges.description: Rod-biased degeneration is one of the major RPGR retinal-dystrophy outcomes.
edges.source: Progressive photoreceptor loss and retinal remodeling

edges.description: RPGR variants can produce macular-predominant atrophic retinal degeneration with visual acuity loss.
edges.source: Progressive photoreceptor loss and retinal remodeling

edges.description: Late retinal remodeling in the retinitis pigmentosa branch includes bone-spicule-like pigment migration.
edges.source: Progressive photoreceptor loss and retinal remodeling

edges.description: RPGR retinal degeneration can produce annular field defects when mid-peripheral retina is preferentially affected.
edges.source: Progressive photoreceptor loss and retinal remodeling

edges.description: Cone-predominant RPGR disease produces light sensitivity along with progressive central visual loss.
edges.source: Progressive photoreceptor loss and retinal remodeling

edges.description: Cone involvement in RPGR disease causes color-vision disturbance.
edges.source: Progressive photoreceptor loss and retinal remodeling

edges.target: RPGR dysfunction at the photoreceptor connecting cilium

nodes.description: Cone dysfunction in RPGR disease can manifest as impaired color vision.

evidence.snippet: Color vision disturbances were reported by all patients with the exception of XRP 235.

nodes.description: High myopia is enriched in ORF15-associated disease and tracks with faster best-corrected visual acuity decline in both rod-cone and cone-predominant branches.

evidence.snippet: RPGR-ORF15 mutations were associated with high myopia (P = 0.01), which led to a faster best-corrected visual acuity decline in patients with RP (P < 0.001) and COD/CORD (P = 0.03).

nodes.description: Some RPGR variants produce macular-predominant atrophic degeneration with progressive loss of visual acuity and comparatively limited peripheral visual impairment.

evidence.snippet: primarily macular atrophy causing

nodes.description: Childhood-onset nyctalopia is the typical presenting symptom of the rod-predominant RP3 branch.

evidence.snippet: Symptoms of nyctalopia typically begin in childhood, with increasing loss of peripheral visual field during teenage years, and progressive central visual loss during the second to fourth decade of life.

nodes.description: Impaired ciliary targeting prevents efficient movement of opsins and other cargo into the photoreceptor outer segment, producing structural disorganization and making both rod and cone photoreceptors vulnerable to degeneration.

evidence.snippet: We also observed disorganized photoreceptor outer-segment morphology and defective trafficking of opsins in the Rpgrko/Y::Cep290rd16/+ mice.

nodes.description: Light sensitivity occurs in cone-predominant RPGR disease along with central visual deterioration and high myopia.

evidence.snippet: mild photophobia, and moderate to high myopia.

nodes.description: Progressive constriction of peripheral vision follows early rod dysfunction in the retinitis pigmentosa branch.

evidence.snippet: Symptoms of nyctalopia typically begin in childhood, with increasing loss of peripheral visual field during teenage years, and progressive central visual loss during the second to fourth decade of life.

nodes.description: Late disease converges on severe rod and cone loss, peripheral-predominant retinal atrophy, and secondary inner-retinal remodeling with reactive gliosis.
nodes.id: Progressive photoreceptor loss and retinal remodeling

evidence.snippet: A histological examination of the donor eye showed disruption of retinal topology and stratification, with a more severe loss found in the peripheral regions. Reactive gliosis was seen in the inner layers of all regions.
Retinitis pigmentosa GTPase regulator (RPGR) gene sequence variants account for the vast majority of X linked retinitis pigmentosa (RP), which is one of the most severe forms of RP.

Variants in the X-linked retinitis pigmentosa GTPase regulator gene (RPGR) and, specifically, in its retinal opening reading frame-15 isoform (RPGRORF15) may cause rod-cone (RCD), cone, and cone-rod dystrophies (CDs and CRDs).
RPGR | HGNC:10295 | RPGR-related retinopathy | MONDO:0100437 | XL | Definitive

nodes.description: RPGR localizes to the photoreceptor connecting cilium, where it helps organize ciliary targeting and transport needed for outer-segment renewal in rods and cones. Loss or destabilization of RPGR creates the shared proximal defect across the RPGR family.
nodes.id: RPGR dysfunction at the photoreceptor connecting cilium

evidence.snippet: RPGR is involved in ciliary function, with ciliary dysfunction now recognised as the mechanism underlying a large proportion of inherited retinal disease.
Ablation of PDE6D blocked ciliary targeting of RPGR.

nodes.description: Progressive loss of central visual acuity occurs across the family and is often earlier and faster in cone- and cone-rod-predominant branches.

evidence.snippet: The probability of being blind (best-corrected visual acuity <0.05) at the age of 40 was 20% and 55% in patients with RP and COD/CORD, respectively.
When analyzing the longitudinal data, a progressive decline in visual acuity (VA) was noted, with more than 60% of the patients reaching VA ≥ 1 LogMar in the best eye after the fifth decade of life.

nodes.description: Annular field defects can occur when RPGR retinal degeneration affects the mid-peripheral retina while sparing more peripheral visual field.

evidence.snippet: For this patient, a wide ring scotoma was found with preservation of the peripheral visual field.

nodes.description: Rod-cone dystrophy is the rod-predominant branch of RPGR-related disease, clinically overlapping retinitis pigmentosa with nyctalopia followed by progressive field constriction and later central visual loss.

evidence.snippet: cause rod-cone (RCD), cone, and cone-rod dystrophies (CDs and CRDs).

nodes.description: Bone-spicule-like pigment in the mid-peripheral and peripheral fundus is a characteristic retinal remodeling feature in RPGR-associated retinitis pigmentosa.

evidence.snippet: Fundus examination revealed typical bone spicule-like pigment in the mid-periphery and periphery in all patients.

nodes.description: Despite a shared ciliary-trafficking defect, RPGR family disease diverges into rod-cone, cone-rod, and cone-dominant phenotypes. Variants toward the 3' half of RPGRORF15 are especially associated with cone-predominant presentations.

evidence.snippet: RPGR-ORF15 mutations were associated with COD/CORD and with a more severe phenotype in RP.
All were located in the 3' half of the RPGRORF15 transcript.
